title: Quiz: Quantum Computing Timeline Estimates
description: Test your understanding of quantum computing timelines and migration planning

questions.question: What does CRQC stand for and what does it mean?

options.text: Cryptographic Random Quantum Computer - a quantum random number generator
Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer - capable of breaking real-world crypto
Certified Reliable Quantum Computer - a verified quantum system
Commercial Ready Quantum Computer - available for purchase

questions.explanation: CRQC stands for Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computer - a quantum computer capable of running Shor's algorithm at scale to break real-world cryptographic systems like RSA-2048.

questions.question: According to Mosca's theorem, when should you start migration if: data lifetime=20 years, migration time=5 years, CRQC estimate=15 years?

options.text: You have plenty of time - wait 10 years
Start migration immediately - you're already 10 years late

questions.explanation: Mosca's theorem: if x + y > z, worry! Here 20 + 5 = 25 > 15. Your data lifetime plus migration time exceeds the time until CRQC. You should have started 10 years ago!

questions.question: Approximately how many logical qubits are needed to break RSA-2048?
questions.explanation: Estimates suggest approximately 4,000 logical qubits are needed to break RSA-2048 using Shor's algorithm. However, each logical qubit requires many physical qubits for error correction.

questions.question: What is the consensus range for most CRQC timeline estimates?
questions.explanation: Most consensus estimates place CRQC around 2035-2045, though optimistic predictions suggest as early as 2030 and conservative estimates extend to 2050+.

questions.question: According to NIST guidance, organizations should wait until quantum computers are built before beginning PQC transition planning.
questions.explanation: False. NIST explicitly states that organizations should NOT wait. They recommend beginning inventory and migration planning immediately, with targets for deprecated algorithms by 2030.

questions.question: What is the 'precautionary principle' in cryptographic migration?

options.text: Only migrate when absolutely necessary to save costs
Migrate early because the cost of being late is potentially catastrophic
Wait for perfect post-quantum algorithms before migrating
Only large organizations need to migrate

questions.explanation: The precautionary principle favors early migration because the cost of being late (compromised data, emergency migration, reputational damage) far outweighs the cost of migrating earlier than strictly necessary.

questions.question: What does NSA CNSA 2.0 require for National Security Systems?

options.text: Post-quantum algorithms (ML-KEM, ML-DSA) by 2030 for software
No changes until quantum computers exist

questions.explanation: NSA CNSA 2.0 requires post-quantum algorithms (ML-KEM/Kyber for key encapsulation, ML-DSA/Dilithium for signatures) by 2030 for software systems and 2033 for hardware systems.

questions.question: Which factors could ACCELERATE quantum computer development? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Error correction breakthroughs (a), new qubit technologies (c), and international competition (d) could all accelerate development. Reduced funding (b) would likely delay progress.
